sentences.text: As--
As I said, I was not going to leave that camp.
Sometimes you go by your instincts.
It's not that I'm smarter than the next guy or anything.
Your instincts dictate you there-- human nature.
It's a law of survival-- what to do and what not to do.
It steers you.
I decided against it.
So I say, if I am-- they blow up, I am dead at once.
And I won't suffer.
That was my luck.
The next day, one of the other leader picks me up, recognized me that I was from the group, from the Organisation Todt.
He says, you always kept yourself good.
Now you're like a skeleton, like the rest of them.
He says, OK.
I'll take you into the kitchen to work-- to peel potatoes.
But if you don't recover within a couple of weeks, I'll finish you off myself to end your suffering.
He will do me a favor.
Well, as you can see, I recovered.
I got into the-- but those were-- the first week was one of my harshest weeks in my life-- physically-- perhaps not mentally, but physically.
I could not sit straight on the bench to peel potatoes.
My spine would not support me.
There was hardly any flesh on my buttocks, so even sitting was hurting.
And I had to peel those potatoes.
But I was extremely hungry.
Like I described before, the hunger is indescribable.
So I would boil-- I would peel a whole gallon can of potatoes, hang it into the stove.
There was a cast-iron stove-- put it on a wire, hang it inside, and boil those potatoes.
I would finish a gallon potatoes.
I was up to my neck full.
I felt nothing can go any further.
Yet, I felt hunger pains.
A few hours later, I would boil again some potatoes.
I could finish only half a can.
I constantly was hungry.
But I was beginning to gain strength.
After everybody left, I will take a broom and sweep up, clean up after myself.
The cook took a liking to me.
He had seen how hard I was trying.
He would call me inside the kitchen and give me a bone with some meat on it, or give me a chunk of artificial honey, which was made out of sugar.
I had to eat it right there and then to finish.
Well after--
No, he was a prisoner, too.
He was also a prisoner.
But he was a cook, you see?
That was a privileged few with a job-- being a cook in a camp.
Well, after a few weeks, I filled out.
Now, some of the prisoners that went-- met with hungry-- like I said, I have seen prisoners peel the plaster off the walls and stuffing it into their mouths.
Others stole some salt.
And they were eating salt to make them thirsty instead of hungry.
Well, those that ate salt usually would die within 12 hours.
They filled themself up with salt.
And within hours, you could see they swell up like balloons.
Their faces blew up.
Where their eyes were, you could see just slits-- and the same with their lips, and thing They laid down to sleep, and never woke up.
It was-- apparently the salt got into their bloodstream, and it killed them.
The next selection is-- I called before the doctor.
He looks at me-- a prisoner doctor.
He says-- he says, you, too, started out on salt?
Because my cheeks were full.
I says, no, doctor.
I don't eat salt.
So he pinches my cheek.
And, you know, if you're normal, it just jumps back like rubber, you know.
He says, you are fat.
Where do you get the food to have cheeks like that?
I say, doctor, I work in the kitchen.
Well, the good times are over.
You've got to go to a labor camp.
Like me, he sent me to a labor camp called Frohburg.
I met most of my buddies there in that camp.
I was put to work with a construction brigade.
Construction-- we were building blocks there for the future concentration camp where I eventually wound up.
That'll be later-- Frohburg.
I'll tell you then.
I was put there to work.
I meet one of the Polacks-- a free laborer who works nearby.
And we start up a conversation.
And he tells me he comes from the vicinity of Czestochowa there, from a village.
I said, oh, that's near us.
I tell him, you know, they're my-- oh, yes, I know your town.
We used to go to buy coal there.
And he-- I says, listen, buddy.
I have a pair of pants I left.
They would probably fit you.
If not, you may have to take them in.
Are they worth anything to you?
And we made a deal for half a loaf of bread.
I hand him over those pants.
I got the half a loaf of bread.
The next day, we come home from work.
The commandant gives a order.
The Nazi comes out with a list.
All the units to remain standing till all the brigades assemble to units.
He lifts up the list, looks up, and calls out my name.
And right away, the first thing that occurs to me-- I sold the Polack the pants.
And like a fool, I gave him my name.
I told him.
They probably caught him with it.
And he told them, probably, that he bought it from me.
From this, they will string me up.
I says, till now luck stayed with me.
Eventually, my luck ran out.
That is the first thing going through my mind.
And he just sizes me up, looks at me, and doesn't say another word.
He lifts up the list and calls another guy.
I think to myself, well, at least if there is going to be a necktie party, I'll have company.
I won't be alone.
He calls a third.
He calls a fourth-- calls out 10 guys.
We look at each other-- all the 10 that used to work in the Organisation Todt.
Out of the 30, exactly 10 survived. 20 died during the epidemic. 2/3 of the camp died in the epidemic.
Out of 3,000, 1,000 remained.
He turns around to the chief of the prisoners-- the altester.
He says, send them to the showers.
Give them clean clothing.
They are going back to the Organisation Todt.
Now, that was paradise after that work-- for two reasons-- easier work-- not as hard-- and a supply of food-- that is, if you are lucky-- [LAUGHS] if you are not caught in the meantime.
Anyways, the next day, we go back.
In the meantime, they had German workers that they employed already.
We are put to work there again.
I'm put to work.
There again, they send me to-- do the accounting-- various.
I do the accounting.
At one time, when the Germans are working, the Germans-- I got to explain that episode-- what happened.
[BACKROUND NOISE] The Germans are loading.
We received a shipment of wooden shoes, or boots, from Holland.
And they were loading them in bags up.
And I was doing the accounting.
And the chief Nazi goes by-- their-- the boss.
And he's-- calls me.
He says, Ziegler, how come you're doing the accounting, not clothing?
I says, my chief, Mr.
Lander, put me here to do that work.
So he right away calls Lander.
Lander!
Come here.
What do you mean of that our people, Germans, do the manual work loading things, and a Jew you put to do the accounting-- the main work?
So he says, Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH], I didn't consider it in that way, that our people do that and he's doing that.
He is a youngster.
And he does more constructive work by doing that than by loading.
By loading, he can't accomplish very much at his size and his age.
And this, he's excellent at it.
You know it yourself, you have no mistake.
You mean to tell me that a Jew is better than a-- he says.
I did not say that.
I said that he is good at it.
And we have no mistakes.
And he does the best work possible by doing that.
So he turns around and says, I will report you.
He says, OK.
If you want to report me, go right ahead.
But then I have something to report too.
And he yells out to him, [GERMAN].
You are KV!
KV in German means [GERMAN]-- or war criminal.
Why did he call him war criminal?
Because the guy was shying away from the army to go to the front line.
Every-- being a young man, he had to go every few months for a doctor-- to-- for a examination-- if he is fit.
And that day, whether he took some medication or something-- whatever he did, he was coming into work holding his stomach.
And they knew it-- all the other Germans.
And they were making-- they were smirking and making remarks about him, how he's, you know, saving himself from the front line.
So when he told him that, the guy ran away and slammed the door behind him.
He never reported him.
He turns around to me.
He says, never mind what he says.
You continue what I told you to do.
At one time, we were slow at the time.
We were opening up a new warehouse.
And they needed an electrician.
So one of the guys started work-- from our group-- was electrician.
So he needed a helper.
I volunteered to help him.
So I was working with him.
And being an eager beaver, I always ask questions.
How does that wire go?
What is the meaning of that [? circuit ?] ?
And how do you make for fuses, a break in the circuit, and so forth?
And he used to explain me everything.
You know, I used to make myself drawings and understand everything for a switch-- the meaning of it.
Anyways, we worked there for about two weeks to three weeks in that warehouse.
He's caught stealing-- they got him.
So of course, if he was caught stealing, he was beaten half to death and sent to the strafcommando, or colonel brigade, where he lasted maybe a week, at most.
So the next day, they're having a meeting-- all the German workers staying around-- and the bosses-- the boss stays.
He says, that son of a bitch.
He says, he had to go steal when we needed him here.
Now we can't finish the work.
I don't know what we'll do.
We have no electrician more.
So I go up to my boss and says, chief, I think I can finish the work.
I learned quite a bit from him.
And he looks at me.
He says, what the hell are you talking-- you worked for two, three weeks with him and you are an electrician already?
I said, no sir.
I'm not electrician.
But I think I can finish the work.
So one of the-- one looks at the other.
He says, you know something?
I think he can.
What have we got to lose?
We are not taking him away from important work.
We have nothing to do anyways.
Let him play around.
And we have a engineer anyways.
The boss will come, and he will inspect the work anyways.
Anyways, they put me to work.
I was so eager to prove myself, I sometimes skipped lunch.
I would eat the piece of bread during lunchtime and be on the stepladder wiring.
I put everything within the fuses, just to the main box, which was high voltage there.
The engineer came to connect.
So he inspects everything.
He says, everything looks fine.
He connects the-- all the switches on.
Everything works fine except one double switch, where it was for two individual lights.
It doesn't work.
It puts on both slides together, I think.
He checks.
He can't find the mistake where it is.
And he gets impatient.
He says, don't worry.
You have done a marvelous job.
I'll be back after lunch.
And I'll find the mistake.
Don't worry.
So all the Germans-- you know-- you see?
You see?
So I walk up to my boss there.
And I say to him, again, chief, if you give me a pencil and paper, I think I can find the mistake what I made.
So I sit down, and I first draw myself a diagram-- how the wires should be for the double switch-- the three wires, the two plus, the one minus, I think.
I open up the junction box, climb up, and look at it.
It doesn't take me five minutes.
I found which wires are crossed.
I untangle it, put it together, switch on the work-- hurry.
That guy, the chief grabs-- no, told me before when I thought.
He says, you find the mistake before the engineer gets back, and you have a whole loaf of bread from me tomorrow morning.
When I finished, and I switch on the light, and it works individually, he grabs himself by the hat.
And he says straight to the other Germans, now those are the Jews!
Their ingenuity and their-- think what they-- how they comprehend things is beyond me, he says.
He says, now when he's fired from here or transferred, he will apply for the job as a electrician.
And believe me, he will do the job, too.
He says, well, Ziegler, you earned the loaf of bread.
And you'll get it tomorrow morning.
But you keep your mouth shut when the engineer comes.
And let me do the talking to him.
To make a long story short, the engineer comes back after lunch.
He says, don't worry, gentlemen.
We will find the mistake.
Everything is fine.
So he goes over to him and says, Mr.
Engineer-- it was-- it's customary in Europe, you know, a guy that has any degree, any title, to call by that title.
Like engineer here, you just call him by his name.
There, you call him Mr.
Engineer, or Mr.
Lawyer, or whatever.
Mr.
Engineer, could you please switch on the lights?
Maybe it fixed itself.
He says, oh, come on.
That's a stupid idea.
He says, please, do me a favor.
Try it.
I have a feeling that maybe it fixed itself.
So he says, I never experienced such stupidity in all my life to think that.
There is no such a thing in electricity.
In electricity, if it's a mistake, you've got to find it and fix it.
And then it works.
But if you want to see, here.
So he turns on the light, and the lights work.
He got mad.
And he said, goddammit.
Ziegler!
Come here.
What's going on here?
What happened?
So I thought-- so I look at my boss.
And he makes the head-- go ahead.
You can tell him now.
So I take out the diagram and show him.
That's how it's supposed to be.
I had those two wires crossed.
So I fixed-- I examined it myself.
I wanted to test myself where I made the mistake-- He says, excellent.
Hold it right here.
I have something for you.
He runs out to his truck, and he brings me in some bread.
I think he had some sandwiches there.
And he has the guard with him, the SS man.
He says, guard, I am giving that prisoner food.
If you want to report me, you can report me.
But he earned it.
So I'm giving it to him.
Now the guard does not want to do-- you know, so many Germans standing around.
He says, if you want to give him, this is your business.
It doesn't concern me.
He just turned around, walked out.
He says, here.
Have it.
You earned it.
Anyways, we went back to work.
Next day, they-- all the Nazis are still standing around-- the Germans.
And that-- my boss comes over to me.
He says, Ziegler, when you worked with that guy-- Grueber was his name-- with Grueber, with the electrician, you were with him constantly.
You mean to tell me you didn't see him steal those pants?
So I says, chief, you think I would permit him to do a thing like that if I see him?
He says, of course not.
You would come and tell me right away.
I says, no, sir.
That I did not say.
I says, I would threaten him, perhaps, that I will tell you.
That would be enough to stop him.
But I would not tell you.
He was still my friend.
And I wouldn't denounce him to you.
So he stood like that for about 30 second.
And he says, I knew you were short.
But I didn't know you were that short.
You are as short as a fox, he says.
And he grabbed me both ears.
Would you have told me that you would tell me, I would hit you so hard across the face that I would probably knock out your teeth.
He says, because I would know that you take me for a fool and you lied to me.
Anyways, later that they go eat.
He takes me aside.
He says, Ziegler, do you consider me a fool?
I says, no sir.
If I would consider you a fool, I would answer like the way you said before.
I would tell you that I would tell you.
But I do not consider you a fool.
He says, I know that you are stealing, because if you would not steal, you would look like the rest of the prisoners there.
You'd be a walking skeleton from the food you are getting.
But please don't let yourself be caught.
I would hate to lose you.
He just grabbed me by the shoulder, patted me on the head, and says go on.
Go to work.
Now he was a decent guy.
His name was Lander.
He was from Baden.
At one time, he mentioned to me during a conversation, when I were-- that most of his business associates were Jews in Berlin before, which meant before the Hitler era.
When Frohburg came I worked there till-- till late fall, which was about the end of September.
1944, which was the final selection.
They selected all the healthy ones, sent them to the concentration camp that we built originally.
The concentration camp was called sportschule, which was about 10 kilometers away from the existing camp.
All the sick and not fit were sent, apparently, to Auschwitz.
We arrived in that camp.
We were kept all day long on the grounds-- taking count of us, issuing us concentration camp numbers, which I received my number, which was 7200688.
My number, by the way, is at the Yad Vashem-- it's a museum-- which I donated personally-- handing it.
I have a picture handing it over to the chairman of the Yad Vashem.
The late Gideon Hausner was the person I give everything.
Yeah, sure.
OK.
The following day, we did some manual work on the grounds.
And we were also assigned to various working groups to go-- factories or construction jobs.
It was wet snow and rain again.
Exposed to the cold, soaking through and through all day long outside, I came home, went into the block that night to sleep.
I could not close my eyes-- constant headache, shivers, and cough.
Every time I coughed, it's like my chest was exploding-- pain in the chest.
In the morning, before going to the group marching off to work, I went into the infirmary.
I reported to the doctor that I am sick.
He examined me and issued me a permit to stay in the infirmary a couple of days.
I found out I had pneumonia.
I was there for about three to four days-- comes a Nazi officer, a doctor going through inspection of the infirmary.
Those that are sick-- collected-- that will have to stay for a week or two, and send away to extermination camp again, which was Auschwitz.
When he came to my bed, he asked me, what's wrong with him?
He told, oh, he has the flu.
He will be out tomorrow-- the doctor said.
In fact, I stayed another three days-- that I was let down-- over a week that I spending.
I was released from there.
I went to work.
I went to work on a construction site building bunkers for the German population in town.
By this time, they expected already that the Russian invasion will start pretty soon.
So again, by being handy, the German foreman noticed.
He puts me in charge to show others how to work.
I get a little bit extra food already.
And I work till-- for another month or so.
Then I am picked, again, to another brigade to work.
He tried to save me.
He just probably took a liking to me.
He had seen the possibilities that I would recover shortly.
And, if he could something help me-- he was also a prisoner, the doctor.
They weren't--
--mean to-- if someone would be chronically sick, he probably wouldn't take the chance.
Because then it would mean his head.
But being that he figured I'll be out in a couple of days-- two, three days-- so he could afford to take the chance.
So it was a Jewish doctor.
Yeah.
Anyways, eventually, I'm transferred to another company-- work in a factory, which was building parts for airplanes.
That was in Langenbielau, from the same prison camp, but another company, a neighboring town, which we were marching every day.
It was about five to six kilometers from the camp-- which we were walking to the camp.
That was already in December '45, I think-- December '44, rather.
I worked there.
Again, I was assigned to the warehouse where I was issuing the raw materials to the various departments.
I had a boss there, a German, national German.
I don't know where, originally, he came from.
He lived, apparently, in Poland before the war, because he used to tell me stories about certain towns in Poland-- for instance, Grodno.
And he knew very much about Jewish life.
So that man apparently-- he was very high strung, always used to scream and shout at us and everything.
But as we know, in January '45, the Russians opened the offensive and crossed the Vistula into Poland, into Western Poland.
And within weeks, they were on German territory already.
Most of the Germans knew that the war is lost.
And my boss started at work.
He was very scared, His conscience must have been bothering him.
He must have-- have committed some horrible crimes, or whatever he had on this conscious.
Because he always talked.
And one time he calls me over during lunchtime.
And he says to me-- and he would address me as Mr.
Ziegler at that time, which was most unusual.
Mr.
Ziegler, sit down here.
Tell me.
What do you think will happen if the Russians come in?
They will probably line me up behind the wall and right away shoot me.
And you guys will probably helped them.
I say, no, sir.
We wouldn't do such a thing.
Of course, if that ever happens, if the Russians should come in, we would protect you.
After all, you are a good man.
I know you are screaming.
And I know why you are screaming, I say-- to alert the guards that you are doing your job so the guards wouldn't come and beat us.
That's why you're screaming at us.
But you never hit anybody or kick anybody like others do.
You are a good man.
He said, you really think so, Mr.
Ziegler?
I says, absolutely.
There is no question in my mind that you are a man with heart.
That day, his lunch went my way.
Mr.
Ziegler, my stomach is bad.
And you are a young fellow.
You eat it.
You need it more than I do.
You're hungry.
You get very little food.
From that day on, every day his lunch went my way.
About the months before the liberation, which was already in April, one morning, he does not come in.
So we asked the guard, where is our boss?
Tell me.
Oh, he passed away last night.
He died.
I am more than sure that he committed suicide.
Because he constantly kept on talking about what the Russians will do to him and what the Russians will do to him.
So I gathered that he must have committed some crime, some murders in the home, [BACKROUND NOISE] the Polish town where he was.
Because he mentioned the town's name and he mentioned just once, even about the Jewish wine that he drank on Passover.
And he says they called it [NON-ENGLISH]-- that that goy-- and he used the term goy-- should not even look at it, because then it's no more Kosher.
Even I didn't know those things at that time, when he explained to me.
He mentioned the town, Grodno, which was a famous town in Poland.
I remember reading a Polish author, a woman, describing a Jewish family in Poland.
She was one of the few that were friendly toward Jews-- describing a rabbinical family of Grodno.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, we worked there till-- we could see the newspapers.

And so when the Germans were reading them-- like that particular fellow used to push the newspaper like towards me so that I could see the headlines or read that they were already on the outskirts of Berlin then.
Then work stopped all at once.

We were just greasing all the machines, dismantling them, building them on trucks, take them on the train station, loading them on train cars to be shipped off, putting on the pilings on top, ripping them up.
And they remained there till the Russians came in.
We stopped a few days before the [BACKROUND NOISE] end of the war, which was already beginning of May.
I remember the last Saturday of the war, which happened to be May the 5th, we were sent to work-- a group of us-- to a sawmill, sorting some lumber things.
And we finished in the early afternoon, about 2 o'clock.
And the guard-- the SS man-- was leading it back to the camp.
As prisoners, we had to walk through town on the pavement-- on the road-- where he would walk on the sidewalk with the gun pointing at us, you know.
And we, the group, walked on the road.
And an elderly German woman walks by and says to the guard, guard, why don't you release those people?
Why are you still leading them?
You know we lost the war.
To us, we were shocked.

You know, a German woman even-- a German woman saying that to a guard-- at best he would arrest her immediately and take her with him, you know, to the camp or maybe even shoot her on the spot for saying something like that.
And he says, I can't release them.
I have my orders.
And we-- we come back to the camp.
And rumors are flying wild already.
We find out that Berlin has fallen.
We hear that Hitler is dead already, which was already.
One guy comes and tells he's just heard from the horse's mouth that a prisoner elder, like the lager altester, was called in to the commandant, to the SS man.
His name was Ulrich-- and was shown a order that he received to take out all the prisoners in the neighboring forest and machine gun them.
